sections.markdown:
# Compiler Macros: A Light Touch

A **compiler macro** is an *optional* shortcut the compiler may apply to calls of a regular function. The function still exists and can be used through `funcall`, `apply`, or `#'`. The compiler macro just lets the compiler rewrite a *direct* call into something faster when the arguments fit a special pattern.

```lisp
(defun cube (x) (* x x x))

(define-compiler-macro cube (&whole form x)
  (if (constantp x)
      `(* ,x ,x ,x)        ; constant fold-friendly
      form))               ; otherwise leave the call alone
```

The second argument here uses `&whole`, which captures the original form so we can return it unchanged when our optimisation doesn't apply. Returning the original form is the polite way to decline.

Common uses:

* Inline a hot path when arguments are constants.
* Specialise a generic call when the argument types are known.
* Issue a warning at compile time for misuse.

When NOT to use:

* As a substitute for `defmacro`. A compiler macro can be skipped (e.g. via `(funcall #'cube ...)`), so the **function** must always be the source of truth.
* For semantics. Only for performance/diagnostics.

sections.explain: The plain function works as expected.

sections.code: (progn (defun cube (x) (* x x x)) (define-compiler-macro cube (&whole form x) (if (constantp x) `(* ,x ,x ,x) form)) (cube 5))
sections.explain: The compiler macro may rewrite (cube 5) to (* 5 5 5). Either way the answer is 125.

sections.code: (progn (defun cube (x) (* x x x)) (define-compiler-macro cube (&whole form x) (if (constantp x) `(* ,x ,x ,x) form)) (funcall #'cube 6))
sections.explain: Going through FUNCALL bypasses any compiler macro — the function is still the authoritative definition.

sections.prompt: Define DOUBLE-IT as a regular function that returns 2*X. Then add a compiler macro that, when X is a numeric constant, expands to (the constant doubled) directly. The function must still return the right answer when called normally.
sections.hint: Function: (* 2 x). Compiler macro: (if (and (constantp x) (numberp x)) (* 2 x) form). The expansion is just a number — no quoting needed because numbers are self-evaluating.
